# Tokyo city guide

## Overview

Tokyo (東京) is a city of contrasts where ancient temples share blocks with neon-drenched electronics districts. It's impossibly clean, absurdly efficient, and home to more Michelin-starred restaurants than any other city on earth. Every neighborhood feels like a different city entirely.

**Best time to visit**: Spring (Mar-Apr) for cherry blossoms, Fall (Oct-Nov) for autumn colors and mild weather.
**Currency**: Japanese Yen (JPY). 150 JPY ≈ 1 USD.
**Language tip**: English signage is common on transit but rare in local spots. Learn basic phrases and use Google Translate's camera feature.

## Neighborhoods

| Neighborhood | Vibe | Best for | Transit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shinjuku (新宿) | Energetic, neon-lit | Nightlife, department stores, Golden Gai | JR Shinjuku Station |
| Shibuya (渋谷) | Young, trendy | Shopping, Shibuya Crossing, Hachiko | JR/Metro Shibuya Station |
| Asakusa (浅草) | Traditional, touristy | Senso-ji Temple, street food, rickshaws | Ginza Line, Asakusa Station |
| Harajuku (原宿) | Colorful, eccentric | Fashion, crepes, Meiji Shrine, Takeshita Street | JR Harajuku Station |
| Akihabara (秋葉原) | Geeky, electric | Anime, manga, electronics, maid cafes | JR Akihabara Station |
| Roppongi (六本木) | Upscale, international | Art museums, fine dining, nightclubs | Hibiya Line, Roppongi Station |
| Ginza (銀座) | Elegant, expensive | Luxury shopping, sushi, galleries | Ginza Line, Ginza Station |
| Shimokitazawa (下北沢) | Indie, bohemian | Vintage shops, live music, coffee | Keio/Odakyu Line, Shimokitazawa Station |

## Food

| Name | Category | Neighborhood | Budget | Description | Must-order |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ichiran Ramen (一蘭) | Ramen | Shibuya | $ | Solo ramen booths with customizable broth. The tonkotsu is silky, rich, and exactly what you need at midnight. | Tonkotsu ramen with extra-firm noodles |
| Tsukiji Outer Market (築地場外市場) | Street food | Tsukiji | $-$$ | The old fish market's outer stalls still serve the freshest seafood in Tokyo. Go early morning. | Tamagoyaki, fresh uni, tuna skewers |
| Afuri (阿夫利) | Ramen | Ebisu | $$ | Yuzu shio ramen that's lighter and more fragrant than the heavy tonkotsu everywhere else. | Yuzu shio ramen |
| Gonpachi (権八) | Izakaya | Roppongi | $$ | The restaurant that inspired Kill Bill's fight scene. Great soba and yakitori in a dramatic interior. | Handmade soba, grilled chicken skewers |
| Sushi Dai (寿司大) | Sushi | Toyosu | $$$ | Worth the early morning queue at Toyosu Market. Omakase from chefs who've been doing this for decades. | Omakase chef's choice set |
| Omoide Yokocho (思い出横丁) | Yakitori alley | Shinjuku | $ | Smoky, cramped alleyway grills near the station. Locals call it "Piss Alley" but the skewers are legendary. | Chicken skin, tsukune (meatball), cold beer |
| Melon Pan Ice Cream | Street food | Asakusa | $ | Warm crispy melon bread filled with cold ice cream. Found near Senso-ji temple. | Melon pan with vanilla ice cream |
| Uobei Sushi (魚べい) | Conveyor sushi | Shibuya | $ | Sushi arrives on a bullet train track to your seat. Order by tablet. Fun, cheap, surprisingly good. | Salmon, tuna, tamago |
| Narisawa | Fine dining | Minato | $$$ | Two Michelin stars, innovative "forest cuisine" that tells a story through each course. Reserve weeks ahead. | Bread of the Forest, seasonal tasting menu |
| Fuunji (風雲児) | Tsukemen | Shinjuku | $ | Tokyo's best tsukemen (dipping noodles). The broth is impossibly thick and flavorful. Always a queue. | Tsukemen with extra chashu |

## Landmarks and activities

| Name | Category | Neighborhood | Budget | Time needed | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Senso-ji Temple (浅草寺) | Culture | Asakusa | Free | 1-2 hours | Tokyo's oldest temple. Walk through the massive Kaminarimon gate and Nakamise shopping street. Best at dawn before crowds. |
| Meiji Shrine (明治神宮) | Culture | Harajuku | Free | 1-2 hours | A peaceful Shinto shrine surrounded by a forested park. The torii gate entrance is massive and calming. |
| Tokyo Skytree (東京スカイツリー) | Views | Sumida | $$ | 1-2 hours | At 634 meters, the tallest structure in Japan. The observation decks offer views on clear days all the way to Mt. Fuji. |
| TeamLab Borderless | Art | Odaiba/Azabudai | $$$ | 2-3 hours | Immersive digital art museum where the installations flow between rooms. No two visits are the same. |
| Shibuya Crossing | Iconic | Shibuya | Free | 30 min | The world's busiest pedestrian crossing. Watch from the Starbucks above or just walk across and feel the energy. |
| Imperial Palace East Gardens | Nature | Chiyoda | Free | 1-2 hours | Beautifully maintained gardens on the grounds of the Imperial Palace. Quiet escape in central Tokyo. |
| Akihabara Electric Town | Culture | Akihabara | Free-$$ | 2-3 hours | Multi-story arcades, anime shops, electronics stores, and maid cafes. Sensory overload in the best way. |
| Shinjuku Gyoen National Garden (新宿御苑) | Nature | Shinjuku | $ | 1-2 hours | One of Tokyo's best parks. Japanese, French, and English garden styles. Peak cherry blossom destination. |
| Tsukiji/Toyosu Fish Market | Culture | Tsukiji/Toyosu | Free | 2-3 hours | Watch the tuna auctions at Toyosu (reserve ahead) and eat at the outer market stalls. Go before 8 AM. |
| Robot Restaurant | Entertainment | Shinjuku | $$$ | 1.5 hours | Neon robots, lasers, taiko drums. Completely absurd and completely unforgettable. Pure Tokyo chaos. |

## Nightlife

| Name | Category | Neighborhood | Budget | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Golden Gai (ゴールデン街) | Bars | Shinjuku | $-$$ | Six narrow alleys packed with over 200 tiny bars, each seating 5-10 people. Some charge a cover, all have personality. |
| Roppongi Hills Area | Clubs/bars | Roppongi | $$-$$$ | Tokyo's international party district. Big clubs, rooftop bars, open late. Draws an expat crowd. |
| Nonbei Yokocho (のんべい横丁) | Bars | Shibuya | $$ | "Drunkard's Alley," a quieter, more intimate version of Golden Gai near Shibuya Station. |
| Womb | Club | Shibuya | $$ | One of Asia's best electronic music clubs. Four floors, top international DJs, world-class sound system. |
| Zoetrope | Whisky bar | Shinjuku | $$-$$$ | Over 300 Japanese whiskies in a tiny bar. The owner knows every bottle's story. Reservations recommended. |

## Transit tips

- **Suica/Pasmo card**: Get one at any station. Tap on/off for trains, buses, and convenience store purchases.
- **JR Pass**: Worth it only if doing day trips to Kyoto, Osaka, or Hakone. Not needed for Tokyo-only trips.
- **Subway**: Two systems (Tokyo Metro and Toei). Both are spotless, punctual to the second, and well-signed in English.
- **Last train**: Around midnight. Miss it and you're stuck until 5 AM. Taxis are expensive late night.
- **Walking**: Tokyo is surprisingly walkable neighborhood by neighborhood. Some of the best discoveries happen on foot.
- **Pro tip**: Download the Japan Travel app by Navitime for route planning. It handles train transfers perfectly.
- **Rush hour**: Avoid the Yamanote Line between 7:30-9:30 AM. Packed beyond belief.

## Useful Japanese phrases

| English | Japanese | Romanization |
|---|---|---|
| Hello | こんにちは | konnichiwa |
| Thank you | ありがとうございます | arigatou gozaimasu |
| Excuse me | すみません | sumimasen |
| Delicious! | おいしい! | oishii! |
| How much? | いくらですか? | ikura desu ka? |
| Where is...? | ...はどこですか? | ...wa doko desu ka? |
| The bill please | お会計お願いします | okaikei onegaishimasu |
| Cheers! | 乾杯! | kanpai! |
